pattern: Obstacles only ever matter through the gaps they leave, so keeping obstacle positions in sorted order and scanning the consecutive gaps up to the query boundary answers every placement check directly.
hints: A block of size sz fits in [0, x] exactly when some gap between two consecutive fixed points (0, the obstacles at or before x, and x itself) is at least sz long.
Keep the obstacle positions in a sorted structure; for a type-2 query, only obstacles at or before x are relevant, and 0 and x act as the two outer boundaries of the search.

Walk the sorted obstacles up to x, tracking the previous boundary and the largest gap seen so far (including the final gap up to x itself); the query answer is just whether that largest gap reaches sz.

edge_inputs.description: statement example with a tight fit
no obstacles placed yet, whole range is open
block exactly fills the gap up to the query boundary, touching is allowed
query boundary itself is not an obstacle, only obstacles strictly before it count
several obstacles then a query needing the widest interior gap

stress.description: random interleaving of obstacle placements and size checks
stress.note: obstacle x values kept unique per the constraint; at least one type-2 query guaranteed

complexity.time: O(n^2) worst case (O(n log n) with a balanced structure)
